LAW & MOTION/DISCOVERY, DEPT. 301, AS TO MARCH-14-2025 HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS BOUTIQUE AIR, INC., AND OPEN TRIP INC. DBA BOUTIQUE AIR'S MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS; THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING WITH MODIFICATIONS. DEFENDANT BOUTIQUE AIR INC. MOVES FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS ON ALL OF PLAINTIFF FABIAN PHILIP COOKE JR.'S CAUSES OF ACTION. THE COURT ACCEPTS ALL FACTS PLEADED AS TRUE, DRAWING REASONABLE INFERENCES IN FAVOR OF THE COMPLAINT, BUT DOES NOT ACCEPT LEGAL CONCLUSIONS (SEE WORD MINUTES FOR COMPLETE RULING). COUNSEL FOR THE PREVAILING PARTY IS TO PREPARE AND SUBMIT A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH REPEATS VERBATIM THE SUBSTANTIVE PORTION OF THE COURT'S RULING, WITH A COVER LETTER REFLECTING COMPLIANCE WITH CRC 3.1312. JUDGE: CHRISTINE VAN AKEN; CLERK: V. DA FONSECA; NOT REPORTED. (301/CVA)

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-01-2025 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. Shana Villoria, Esq. of Lipeles Law Group (tel: 310-322-2211) for the Plaintiff.
Atty. Lindsay Rosas, Esq. of Fisher & Phillips LLP (tel: 213-403-96380) for Defendants Boutique Air, Inc., Open Trip Inc. DBA Boutique Air.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANTS BOUTIQUE AIR, INC., AND OPEN TRIP INC. DBA BOUTIQUE AIR's DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT.

Matter discussed. The Court notes the declaration from Plaintiff's counsel was pending in the electronic queue. The Court therefore vacates the order to show cause. The tentative ruling is adopted as modified.

Boutique Air Inc.'s demurrer to plaintiff Fabian Phillip Cooke's cause of action for wrongful termination in violation of public policy is sustained with leave to amend. Cooke does not say what policy, implemented by whom, was violated by Boutique's decision to terminate him when he refused to return to work before testing negative for COVID after a purported exposure. The court disagrees with Boutique that the policy must be embodied in statute or a constitutional provision. (See Foley v. Interactive Data Corp. (1988) 47 Cal.3d 654, 669 [acknowledging split in courts of appeal and leaving the question open].) With respect to COVID, relevant policies were issued by federal, state, and local health authorities. But Cooke must plead what the policy is and how Boutique violated it; otherwise the complaint does not serve its notice function.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-01-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. Shana Villoria, Esq. of Lipeles Law Group (tel: 310-322-2211) for the Plaintiff.
Atty. Lindsay Rosas, Esq. of Fisher & Phillips LLP (tel: 213-403-96380) for Defendants Boutique Air, Inc., Open Trip Inc. DBA Boutique Air.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT BOUTIQUE AIR, INC., AND OPEN TRIP INC. DBA BOUTIQUE AIR's MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT.

Matter discussed. The Court notes the declaration from Plaintiff's counsel was pending in the electronic queue. The Court therefore vacates the order to show cause. The tentative ruling is adopted as modified.

Defendant Boutique Air Inc.'s motion to strike portions of plaintiff Fabian Phillip Cooke's First Amended Complaint (FAC) is granted in part and denied in part as stated herein.

Boutique seeks to strike stray references to FEHA and FEHA requirements in light of the court's prior order of March 18, 2025, sustaining its demurrer to FEHA causes of action without leave to amend. The court grants the motion; without a FEHA cause of action there is no proper basis to allege FEHA violations. The following is stricken from the FAC: a following portion of paragraph 19 ("Employers refused to discuss any reasonable accommodation or engage in any interactive process for Plaintiff's perceived risk of illness and . . . ."); paragraph 38; and paragraph 65(d).

Boutique seeks to strike other portions of the FAC that, it argues, violate the sham pleading doctrine. The court denies the motion. Most of the allegations that Boutique targets do not contradict anything alleged in the prior pleading. Even as to the allegations that are arguably in tension with the original complaint - such as that Boutique told him the captain in fact had COVID (in the prior version, Ryan Nestor of Boutique said the captain did not have COVID), that Boutique flew Cooke home after he believed he was exposed to COVID, and that Cooke used an app to report his absence instead of calling - are not material to the court's demurrer ruling. To explain: the sham pleading doctrine prohibits a party from avoiding a demurrer or summary judgment by omitting or falsifying facts in its pleading. Under the doctrine, the court may consider earlier pleadings to avoid a fraud on the court. (See, e.g., Larson v. UHS of Rancho Springs, Inc. (2014) 230 Cal.App.4th 336, 344 ["A plaintiff may not avoid a demurrer by pleading facts or positions in an amended complaint that contradict the facts pleaded in the original complaint or by suppressing facts which prove the pleaded facts false. [Citation.] Likewise, the plaintiff may not plead facts that contradict the facts or positions that the plaintiff pleaded in earlier actions or suppress facts that prove the pleaded facts false."].) Here, however, none of the allegations Boutique seeks to strike is material to the demurrer ruling.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-05-2025 Continued To Jul-01-25 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 301
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANTS BOUTIQUE AIR, INC., AND OPEN TRIP INC. DBA BOUTIQUE AIR's DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

The court continues hearing on the demurrer on its own motion to July 1, 2025. The Rosas Declaration establishes that plaintiff did not satisfy his obligation during meet and confer efforts to "provide legal support for its position that the pleading is legally sufficient or, in the alternative, how the complaint, cross-complaint, or answer could be amended to cure any legal insufficiency." (CCP 430.41(a)(1).) The court orders plaintiff to meet and confer in good faith concerning the issues defendant has identified and to file a declaration with the court by no later than five court days in advance of the continued hearing date describing his compliance with the cited statute. If the parties' meet and confer efforts result in an amendment to the operative complaint, the parties are ordered to advise the court five court days in advance of the continued hearing date as the demurrer will be mooted by the filing of an amended complaint.

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Defendants Responses To Plaintiffs First Request For Production Of Documents
Off Calendar Mar-10-2025 Off Calendar

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Defendants Responses To Plaintiff?S Form Interrogatories, Set One
Off Calendar Mar-10-2025 Off Calendar

Notice Of Motion And Mtion To Compel Attendance And Testimony Of The Person Most Knowledgeable For Boutique Air, Inc.
Off Calendar Mar-18-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF FABIAN COOKIE's Motion To Compel Attendance And Testimony Of The Person Most Knowledgeable For Boutique Air, Inc.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Moving party Fabian Phillip Cooke seeks an order compelling Boutique Air's person most qualified to appear for deposition and produce documents. The parties' moving papers demonstrate that Cooke noticed the deposition, Boutique Air objected and was not quick to provide new dates, Cooke renoticed the deposition, Boutique Air again objected, and Cooke filed this motion to compel. Boutique Air's failure to provide alternative dates for over a month after objecting on December 5, 2024, was without substantial justification. But the court cannot order compliance with Cooke's PMQ notice and accompanying request for documents. First, the court has granted Boutique Air's motion for judgment on the pleadings as to Cooke's disability- and sick-leave related causes of action. Cooke has leave to amend as to only one of them. Cooke's PMQ notice includes topics and document requests related to causes of action that are no longer part of the case. Second, Cooke's motion to compel fails to set forth specific facts showing good cause justifying the production of documents sought. (CCP 2025.450(b)(1).) The court denies the motion to compel and finds that neither party acted with substantial justification. The court does not award sanctions.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)
